summary:
The Golden Age of Piracy (c. 1680–1730 AD) was the period of most intense piratical activity in the Caribbean and Atlantic Ocean — an era when hundreds of pirates operating from bases in the Bahamas, Madagascar, and West Africa attacked merchant shipping and briefly made oceanic trade insecure across a vast area. The Golden Age emerged from the aftermath of the [[event:2347|War of the Spanish Succession]], which left thousands of skilled sailors and naval veterans unemployed; the Caribbean's geography, with its numerous islands, hidden harbors, and rich trade routes, made it ideal for piracy. Figures like Blackbeard (Edward Teach), Bartholomew Roberts, Calico Jack, and Anne Bonny became celebrated — and feared — throughout the Atlantic world.

The social dynamics of piracy in this era have attracted considerable historical attention. Pirate ships operated according to democratic principles remarkable for their time: captains were elected by crews, profits were divided according to agreed shares, and wounded pirates received compensation from a common fund — a governance system more equitable than most 18th-century institutions. This has led some historians to interpret piracy as a form of social protest by men who had experienced the brutal exploitation of the merchant and naval service. The British government, using naval power and the courts (the Vice-Admiralty Courts were empowered to try pirates without jury in colonial ports), suppressed the Golden Age piracy by the 1730s through executions — Bartholomew Roberts's death in 1722 and the Nassau trials are commonly cited as the era's end. The cultural legacy of Caribbean piracy — perpetuated in literature, film, and folklore — has made it one of history's most romanticized criminal phenomena.
